paragraphs.body: Chinese Culture and Tradition to Me

“How much money did you get for Chinese New Year?” my friends would always ask, their voices full of excitement as we gathered to compare our hauls. Everyone was eager to show off their red envelopes, as if the amount inside was some kind of competition. Was it really the crisp bills that made us so excited, or was it just the thrill of having the biggest amount of change? For most of them, it was probably the money, but for me, it was something far deeper. Every red envelope I received carried more than just cash; it carried the weight of tradition, a connection to the generations before me. It was a symbol of my family’s culture, passed down year after year, bringing us together in a way that was far more important than the money inside.

Yes of course money makes me happy, like who doesn’t want free money? However, it isn’t just about getting rich, it’s about experiencing the tradition to its fullest and meaningful way. Celebrating Chinese New Year just for the money misses the entire point, and I feel like that’s something a lot of kids these days don’t realize. As the years go by, the essence of the holiday is slowly fading, especially for Chinese families living in America. The whole point of Chinese New Year is far more than just sitting down eating food and getting money. It rather about assisting your grandma in the kitchen, helping your mom clean the house, and even forcing yourself to wear that dress you don’t want to wear because it shows respect for the occasion and the generations before you. Things have changed dramatically in recent years. Rather than celebrating their cultures, it has become so common to see kids glued to screens, also often isolating themselves in their rooms for the entirety of the holidays. There’s been a huge disconnection between people and culture and it’s no wonder why our traditions are dying. However, it’s not only the traditions but also the spoken language.

When I moved from New York to Pennsylvania, I was struck by how many people around me only spoke English as their language. While international students offered a different perspective, I felt a growing concern because this was something outside my social norm. Nothing against my roommate, but she is half Cambodian and half Vietnamese. I often ask people about their native languages because I love connecting with my peers in Chinese. However, when I asked my roommate if she spoke Vietnamese she replied with no, I asked if her mother spoke it, she also replied with no. When I asked another friend who was in the same dorm floor as me, Jessica, who is Korean, the same question, she replied simply with "no." This realization was a cultural shock for me. As harsh as it may sound, it’s clear that our generation has become more Americanized than before. Since arriving here, I haven’t spoken a single word of Chinese, maybe I simply haven’t met the right people. Maybe I was the only one serious about traditional values and cultural appreciation.

I wonder how many people think about the fact that once their grandma passes away, they might never get to taste her cooking again. The dishes she made had a unique flavor that made you instantly recognize them as her own, no one could master them like she could. If seven different dishes were placed on a table, you would automatically know which one was your grandma’s just by the taste. Every family has its own distinct food traditions, and for me, this feeling is constant. I love my grandma’s food so much that I can’t help but worry: what will happen if I can no longer enjoy those flavors that remind me of her love and care? Not only that but will I ever hear her talk to me the way she did? Will I ever hear the phrases "不听老人言，
吃亏在眼前” or even “女儿是妈妈的贴心小棉袄” when she’s gone? Will she make me smile the way she did when she was here?

Language and traditions may not hit as hard to some individuals, but it means a lot to me. The language I speak is more than just my cultural identity; it is more of a vital communication between me and my grandma. The many moments I spent speaking mandarin to her has built an unbreakable relationship between us sharing past lives, traditional well beings and important values. Speaking to her while she’s cooking Chinese New Year dinner will always be my favorite; she always shares her recipes and hopes that one day it’ll be passed down to me. It’s truly heartbreaking when we see as generations grow, the culture that we once grew up in dies down. I thought to myself one day, will my kids celebrate Chinese New Year with their kids the way I did with my family if I never taught them about Chinese culture? Hearing about people my age and race not being able to speak their traditional language makes me wonder. The values my grandma taught me showing respect and resilience as well as keeping family as the top priority is something I can never forget. Constantly when I accidently blurt out that I hated my brothers, my grandma always steps in reassuring me that they are my blood brothers nothing will rip us apart, family is forever. Family is a strong topic in my household, I’ve been taught that family is everything that I’ll always need. It nurtures this bond within me and my relatives to keep the traditions alive, showing me that language can be one of the powerful ways of passing down our culture. 1. To speak mandarin means knowing the warmth you feel in your grandma’s voice as she unfolds the past tales of her life in Fuzhou while you snuggle in your seat eating the pork filled dumplings she made with her loving hands 2. To live in China means to go through pain and sacrifices in order to gain respect and privilege that’ll meet the expectations of the elderly 3. To live in this culture means knowing you can sense the feeling of pride and happiness when they tell you that they’re finally proud of you 4. To speak mandarin means knowing the joy and cheerfulness as everyone gathers on the New Year table eating all the delicacies that grandma and grandpa made 5. To speak a language means knowing that once grandma passes away, you’ll never get to taste the food made by her again and you’ll soon forget the taste of home 6. To live in a Chinese household means knowing the excitement of gathering families for Lunar New Year, where we share hugs and red envelopes filled with wishes for good luck.
